serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked prawns in a Singaporean meal is approximately 100g, equivalent to 6–8 medium-sized prawns served as a side dish or component of a mixed plate.

nutrient_source.title: Prawns — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prawns (虾), cr_id G00011065, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for cooked shrimp/prawns as a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for cooked prawns exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood (7.92g carbohydrate/100g, predominantly from glycogen). GI is estimated at approximately 5 based on analogous high-protein seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Glycemic load per 100g serving = (5 × 7.92) / 100 = 0.40, classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 502mg/100g with low potassium offset (184mg/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.4g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn portions and request preparation without added salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium condiments such as XO sauce or black bean sauce, as the baseline sodium of 502mg/100g already approaches 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit per 100g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (13.24g/100g), low GI (~5), moderate carbohydrate (7.92g/100g) predominantly from glycogen
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawns are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their high protein content, very low glycemic index, and low sugar (2.96g/100g); however, sweet or starch-thickened prawn preparations such as honey prawns or sweet-and-sour prawns should be avoided as they significantly increase the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 119mg/100g; low saturated fat (0.26g/100g); high sodium (502mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns contain moderate dietary cholesterol (119mg/100g) but very low saturated fat (0.26g/100g), making them acceptable in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions; the high sodium content (502mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern and preparation method should minimise added salt and high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, lean seafood protein, calcium 58mg/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prawns are a lean seafood with low saturated fat and provide calcium (58mg/100g) relevant to bone protection in rheumatoid arthritis; deep-fried preparations should be avoided due to advanced glycation end-products, and cholesterol content (119mg/100g) warrants moderation if on lipid-affecting RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawns (虾, Xia) are a widely consumed crustacean seafood in Singaporean Chinese cuisine, served across hawker centres, zi char stalls, and home kitchens. They are typically prepared by stir-frying, steaming, boiling, or deep-frying, and appear in dishes such as butter prawns, garlic prawns, chilli prawns, and prawn noodle soup. The flesh is firm, slightly sweet, and succulent when cooked correctly. Nutritionally, prawns are a high-protein food (13.24g/100g) with very low fat (1.22g/100g) and moderate carbohydrate (7.92g/100g), primarily from natural glycogen. They provide calcium (58mg/100g), potassium (184mg/100g), magnesium (25mg/100g), and iron (0.41mg/100g). The sodium content is elevated at 502mg/100g, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension. Cholesterol is moderate at 119mg/100g. The glycemic index is very low (~5), making prawns suitable for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without sweet or starch-thickened sauces. Prawns are a shellfish allergen and are not suitable for individuals with crustacean allergies. They are halal and gluten-free in their natural form but may acquire allergens through preparation. In Singapore, prawns are a staple protein across Chinese, Malay, and Indian culinary traditions.
common_user_queries: Are prawns good for weight loss?
What is the protein content of prawns?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Prawns (cr_id G00011065) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean foods. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this record (G00011065) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.26g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) as a secondary reference, proportionally scaled to the HPB total fat value of 1.22g/100g. GI value of approximately 5 is an analogous estimate for high-protein seafood from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for cooked prawns was found in the approved GI database. All core macros (protein_g 13.24, fat_g 1.22, carbohydrate_g 7.92) are non-zero and sourced from HPB.
